explanation: The natural language statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' encodes the implication: whenever prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same position. In LTL, this is written as 'globally, if prop_0, then prop_1'.
The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' means that whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is expressed as globally (G), if prop_0 then prop_1.

The sentence 'If prop_0, prop_1' is interpreted as: whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that moment (an implication). In LTL, this is globally (G), since we want this property to hold at all times: G (prop_0 -> prop_1).

The statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' translates to the implication: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this can be expressed globally as 'always (if prop_0 then prop_1)'.

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' is an implication: whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time. In LTL, this is written as 'Globally (prop_0 -> prop_1)', meaning everywhere in time, if prop_0 occurs, then prop_1 holds as well.

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' expresses a conditional logic: whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at that point. In LTL, this is captured globally by ensuring that it is always the case that if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 also holds.

The natural language statement 'If prop_0, prop_1' is interpreted as 'whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at the same time.' This is a classic implication in temporal logic, generally expressed globally.

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' in natural language is a conditional statement, which in LTL translates to 'globally, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must also hold at the same time.' This is expressed as 'G (prop_0 -> prop_1)'.

The phrase 'If prop_0, prop_1' means that, whenever prop_0 is true, prop_1 must also be true at the same time. This is represented as globally (G): for all points in time, if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 must hold.
The phrase is ambiguous, but assuming it means 'if prop_0 holds, then prop_1 holds,' we encode this as a global implication: 'whenever prop_0 holds, prop_1 must also hold at that time.'
